name: Threshold step
description: A raised stone or hardwood step set immediately outside the door, one or two blocks tall and at least as wide as the leaf it serves. Its first job is practical: it lifts the doorsill above the splash line of rain, mud, and trampled snow, so the sealed interior floor stays dry and the door bottom does not rot. Its second job is psychological: by forcing a small, deliberate rise of the foot, it marks the boundary between exterior and interior, gives the visitor a half-second pause to wipe boots and shift posture, and signals to the inhabitant that someone is arriving. Found across virtually every vernacular tradition — medieval stone stoops, Mediterranean limestone sills, rustic timber-framed farmhouse steps — and combinable with any richer entry template above it.
